claim_status: No-claim profile/session and storage-backend source observations only; these sources are not a Turing profile format, migration protocol, credential vault, sync design, data-loss result, real-user-data approval, production readiness decision, or implementation evidence.

sources.title: Storage Standard
sources.observation: The Storage Standard defines storage keys and storage buckets around web-origin identity and related partitioning concepts.
sources.decision_consequence: Turing must preserve origin and partition identity at the web-storage boundary and keep browser-owned profile, Space, and session identities distinct; the standard does not define Turing's profile format, credentials, sync, or recovery policy.

sources.title: Indexed Database API 3.0
sources.observation: IndexedDB 3.0 defines transaction commit and abort behavior and exposes default, strict, and relaxed durability hints; strict may flush operating-system I/O before completion at a cost in latency and power.
sources.decision_consequence: A Turing data-safety packet must assign durability classes, retain abort/commit outcomes, and test crash or power-loss behavior; a successful completion event or durability hint is not proof of user-data durability.

sources.title: Clear-Site-Data
sources.observation: Clear-Site-Data defines origin-scoped clearing categories for cache, cookies, DOM-accessible storage, and execution contexts, with incomplete-clearing and privacy considerations.
sources.decision_consequence: Turing must test and report origin clearing separately from browser profile deletion, private-session close, credential revocation, cache pressure, and migration; clearing one class cannot be represented as deleting all browser-owned state.

sources.title: Security and Privacy Questionnaire
sources.observation: The W3C security and privacy guidance treats local browser storage mechanisms as potential sources of persistent user information and asks user agents to consider clearing and privacy effects.
sources.decision_consequence: Profile/session design must document persistence, clearing, retention, export, private-session exclusion, and identifier-remanence risks; the questionnaire does not approve a Turing privacy policy or real-profile handling.

sources.title: Atomic Commit In SQLite
sources.observation: SQLite documents journal-based atomic commit across interruption and power failure, with storage flushes and hardware/filesystem assumptions as part of the mechanism.
sources.decision_consequence: A Turing persistence experiment must record journal/WAL mode, sync policy, filesystem and hardware assumptions, checkpoint behavior, and recovery outcomes; atomic-commit documentation does not prove the selected profile backend or platform configuration is durable.

sources.title: How To Corrupt An SQLite Database File
sources.observation: SQLite identifies unsafe file access, locking failures, failed sync, filesystem or hardware faults, and configuration errors as corruption or durability risks, while distinguishing consistency from durable persistence.
sources.decision_consequence: Profile/session tests must exercise concurrent access, journal pairing, lock and sync failures, direct-file interference controls, corruption detection, quarantine, and the difference between a consistent rollback and a durably committed record.

sources.title: LMDB Python Documentation
sources.observation: LMDB documents memory-mapped copy-on-write transactions, one simultaneous writer, many readers, reader lifetime effects, map-size limits, and the distinction between atomicity/consistency/isolation and durability when synchronization is relaxed.
sources.decision_consequence: A Turing comparison must record map sizing, reader lifetime, remap and resize behavior, writer serialization, synchronization policy, platform virtual-memory constraints, and recovery outcomes; a memory-mapped design is not approved by these observations.

sources.title: Transactions
sources.observation: RocksDB documents a log-oriented transaction family with explicit transaction, conflict, recovery, and compaction concerns that differ from a single relational profile database.
sources.decision_consequence: A Turing comparison must measure log growth, conflict and retry behavior, replay, compaction, checkpoint, corruption, and bounded recovery separately; this upstream documentation does not approve RocksDB as a Turing dependency or profile backend.

sources.title: Write-Ahead Log
sources.observation: RocksDB documents write-ahead-log identity and lifecycle concerns for recovery and durability, including the relationship between log retention and flushing or checkpoint behavior.
sources.decision_consequence: A Turing log candidate must pin log and checkpoint policy, flush assumptions, retention and deletion rules, corruption handling, replay bounds, and cleanup evidence rather than treating a WAL file as proof of durable user data.

sources.title: Flushing System-Buffered I/O Data to Disk
sources.observation: Windows documents that ordinary writes are system-buffered and that applications must use explicit flush or write-through controls when persistence of buffered data matters; flush frequency also affects performance.
sources.decision_consequence: Windows fault tests must capture the selected flush/write-through policy, error handling, ordering, latency, and recovery result. A successful write or close must not be counted as persistent profile durability.

sources.title: Credential Manager
sources.observation: Windows Credential Manager is a protected operating-system facility for storing and retrieving credentials, distinct from ordinary application profile records.
sources.decision_consequence: A Windows profile/session packet must record the vault boundary, target identity, access/error behavior, user or broker mediation, deletion and logout semantics, and the result when the vault is unavailable; session export, migration, diagnostics, and backups must exclude secret material.

sources.title: Keychain Services
sources.observation: Apple Keychain Services provides protected storage and controlled access for passwords, keys, certificates, and other secrets outside ordinary application documents.
sources.decision_consequence: A macOS profile/session packet must identify the keychain access boundary, access-group or entitlement assumptions, authorization and locked-state behavior, deletion/logout semantics, and the result when access is denied; profile migration and diagnostics must not copy secret values.

sources.title: Secret Service API
sources.observation: The Secret Service specification defines a D-Bus API for storing and retrieving secrets through a desktop service, with collection, item, prompt, and session concepts separate from browser-owned records.
sources.decision_consequence: A Linux profile/session packet must record the service/backend and session or prompt behavior, identity and permission boundary, locked/unavailable/error paths, deletion semantics, and unsupported desktop environments; profile export, migration, and diagnostics must exclude secret material.

unsupported: No executable profile, Space, session, snapshot, or migration schema is approved by this manifest.
No migration, rollback, crash, power-loss, quota, corruption, deletion, export, private-session, credential, or real-profile execution evidence exists.

No production profile format, sync protocol, credential storage, data-loss safety, user-data handling readiness, release-path approval, or owner-reviewed PB-016 readiness decision follows from these sources.
No profile/session, privacy, storage, migration, compatibility, production, Chrome-class, performance, or implementation claim follows from this manifest.
